name: Aarya Khanna
about: I'm a data scientist passionate about using data and machine learning techniques to make an impact in helping solve critical problems. My research interests include high-dimensional statistics, clustering, and manifold learning.

highlights: Built classification algorithms and data pipelines to predict and optimize flavor performance using ingredient ratios and trial metadata.
Restructured trial processes to boost statistical power by blocking on key features correlating with historical flavor preferences.
Used I-optimal trial selection to identify which trials to prioritize for maximum information gain and reduced uncertainty in the model feedback loop.
experience.software: Python (Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib), SQL, Microsoft Office (Excel, Word, PowerPoint)
experience.methods: XGBoost/SHAP, Random Forest, logistic/linear regression, K-means clustering, ANOVA/chi-square tests, regularization, dimension reduction (UMAP, PCA)

experience.organization: UCLA Artificial Intelligence & Cultural Heritage Laboratory
highlights: Clustered questions from 15,000+ large-scale text documents into groups of semantic similarity, leveraging automated pronoun disambiguation.
Built a hybrid pipeline: initial embedding screenings to filter unlikely matches, then a cross-attention model with the right context window to maximize cluster assignment quality.

Automated cluster quality evaluation by comparing each vector’s distance to its cluster centroid—reverse-engineering a “cluster theme”—then used it as a baseline for an LLM to grade compactness with contextual reasoning.
experience.software: Python (PyTorch, Pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, matplotlib, NLTK, Hugging Face, SBERT), Linux
experience.methods: K-means / HDBSCAN / DBSCAN clustering, logistic/linear regression, LLMs, API text embeddings, dimension reduction (UMAP/PCA), silhouette scores

programming.languages: Python, SQL, R, C++, Java, Tableau, MATLAB
programming.libraries: TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, Pandas, NumPy, LangChain, Pinecone, Streamlit, SBERT/OpenAI

projects.title: Transit Deserts: Who the Los Angeles Metro System Leaves Behind
bullets: Planning-oriented spatial analysis for LA County: tract job accessibility, transit desert mapping, and rail corridor screening.
GTFS + census/LEHD/ACS; graph Laplacian effective resistance; reproducible pipeline with figures 01–07.
Screening heuristic (not a ridership forecast): high endpoint activity × structural bottleneck on the current rail network.

intro: This work maps where LA County tracts have low transit-connected job opportunity within 30 minutes, labels the bottom 20% as transit deserts, and relates outcomes to income. It then builds a rail-only graph from GTFS, uses effective resistance between stations to measure network bottlenecks, weights stations by nearby population and jobs, and ranks candidate station-to-station corridors — a transparent screening tool for planning conversations, not a detailed engineering or ridership model.
Skim the sections below for the problem framing, data, methods, modeling, and results-driven visualization gallery.

sections.title: Purpose & questions
paragraphs: The analysis answers three linked questions: (1) Where do census tracts have low job accessibility by transit within a fixed time budget? (2) Where are transit deserts — tracts in the worst tail of that accessibility distribution? (3) Among candidate rail corridor segments between high-activity stations, which pairs score highest on a simple structural × activity proxy: endpoint activity × how ‘electrically distant’ the pair is on today’s rail graph?

It is explicitly not a ridership forecast, operations model, or alignment design. It is a reproducible screening layer that connects tract-level need, network geometry, and land-use intensity near stations.

sections.title: Data & geography
paragraphs: Unit of analysis: census tracts clipped or filtered to LA County (GEOID prefix 06037). Map figures use a fixed bounding box over the LA region (config-driven).

Inputs: tract boundaries (Census shapefile); LEHD WAC job counts aggregated to tract (C000); ACS for population and median income; GTFS feeds for transit — stops in the bbox for accessibility, and rail route types for the metro graph.

sections.title: Tract job accessibility (≤30 min) & transit deserts
paragraphs: For each residential tract origin, the model estimates how many jobs in other tracts are reachable within ≤30 minutes using a simplified walk–transit–walk chain: nearest GTFS stop to origin and destination tract centroids, Euclidean walk legs at walk speed, great-circle transit between stops at an effective transit speed, plus a fixed wait penalty. Total time is compared to a 30-minute threshold; accessibility is the cumulative opportunity count of jobs across destinations (gravity-free).

Transit desert is defined as tracts at or below the 20th percentile of tract accessibility (job counts reachable in 30 minutes) — a relative, regional cut within the modeled snapshot. The accessibility layer is intentionally a broad spatial prior: it does not encode transfers, headways, or mode choice.

sections.title: Metro rail graph & effective resistance
paragraphs: Rail-only subgraph: GTFS route types in the tram/subway/rail family; nodes are stops in the bbox; edges connect consecutive stops along trips with weight = great-circle meters; transfer edges link nearby stops within a short distance (e.g. 150 m). The largest connected component is retained so resistance is well-defined.

Edge conductance is taken as c_ij = 1/(d_ij + 1) with distance d_ij in meters. Build the weighted Laplacian L = D − A and use the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse L⁺ of L. Effective resistance between stations a and b is:
bullets: Higher R_eff means the pair is poorly connected by parallel paths on the current topology — an electrical-network analogy.
A separate diagnostic f^T L f (mean tract accessibility near stations) summarizes graph-smoothness of accessibility; it is not the corridor ranking score.

sections.title: Station weights & corridor impact score
paragraphs: Around each graph station, population and jobs are aggregated within an 800 m buffer (tract centroids via spatial index). Station weight is sqrt(max(pop,1) × max(jobs,1)) — a smooth, strictly positive activity proxy, not observed ridership.

Candidates: top stations by weight; random station pairs (seeded) under a max great-circle separation cap. Scoring: demand_proxy = weight_a × weight_b; impact_score = demand_proxy × R_eff(a,b). Buffer statistics along the corridor (e.g. mean accessibility, desert share) are saved for context but are not multiplied into impact_score (per the project methodology).

sections.title: Pipeline outputs & example run
paragraphs: The pipeline writes processed tract accessibility GeoJSON/Parquet, summary CSVs (run KPIs, graph summary), corridor_priorities.csv with ranked segments, and a matplotlib figure suite (01–07).

One completed run (order-of-magnitude): on the order of ~2,500 tracts; ~23% flagged as desert (bottom-20% rule can produce ~20% with ties/edge effects); rail graph on the order of ~67 nodes and ~67 edges in that run; raw impact magnitudes are huge — use ranks and normalized plots, not raw scale, for intuition.

sections.title: Limitations
bullets: Accessibility is a simplified OD-time approximation (nearest stop, no realistic routing).
Desert is relative to the modeled region/year — not an absolute service standard.
Corridor sampling is not exhaustive; ranks are within the generator, not a global optimum over all alignments.
Impact score is a screening heuristic — not a mandate to build.
R_eff depends on how the rail graph is coded (modes, transfers, largest component).

visuals.caption: 01 — Accessibility jobs (left) and binary transit desert (bottom 20%). The problem map: spatial inequality of job opportunity under the simplified transit model.
02 — Median income vs accessibility; desert tracts highlighted. Descriptive equity lens — not causal.
03 — Rail edges and station markers scaled by log-scale demand proxy. Where rail overlaps population–jobs intensity near stops.
04 — Top corridor segments by impact score on a basemap; candidate priority geography (not a final alignment).
05 — Decomposition: demand vs R_eff vs impact (each normalized separately) for transparency.
06 — Top corridors over desert hatched tracts: fusion of priority segments and underserved areas.
07 — Report-style table: stations, length, demand, R_eff, impact — matches CSV for communication.

frameworks: Entry: scripts/run_la_analysis.py · config: src/config.py · core: src/la_analysis.py, src/transport_graph.py, src/viz_la.py

projects.title: TwistEd
bullets: Real-time weather alerts and safest recommended travel routes.
Interactive chatbot for historical weather questions.
Educational section on severe weather types and precautions.

intro: TwistEd combines live hazard data with routing and an educational layer so users get alerts, safer paths, and context on what the risk actually means.
The assistant path uses retrieval over historical weather records so answers stay grounded in real events rather than generic text.
detail.stack: Client requests weather alerts and route preferences; backend aggregates API feeds, scores edges on risk, and serves a small RAG-style chat index over curated historical snippets.
detail.methods: Heuristic or learned route scoring under alerts; optional NLP intent routing for the chatbot; structured cards for the education module.
frameworks: Vector store or BM25 for chat retrieval

visuals.caption: UI mock or alert + route screenshot (add file under /images/).

projects.title: CoursePlanner AI Chatbot
bullets: Uses UCLA’s Developer API with LangChain and Pinecone to build optimized course plans from natural language.
Contextualizes top matches when justifying specific course recommendations.

intro: Students describe goals and constraints in natural language; the system queries UCLA course data, embeds descriptions, and retrieves tight candidate sets before the LLM explains tradeoffs.
Pinecone stores embedding-indexed course chunks so recommendations stay specific to UCLA offerings and update when the catalog changes.
detail.stack: LangChain orchestration (tools for catalog lookup), embedding model for course text, Pinecone for similarity, and a policy prompt that forces citations to retrieved rows.
detail.methods: Top-k retrieval with MMR or diversity, optional re-ranking, and structured JSON outputs for “plan” objects the UI can render.

visuals.caption: Example chat turn with retrieved course cards (screenshot).

projects.title: Analogy LLM
bullets: Building an LLM that explains mathematical concepts in the context of another field through strong analogies.

intro: The goal is pedagogy: map abstract math to a domain the learner already trusts (biology, economics, music, etc.) while preserving definitions and not overstating isomorphism.
Training / prompting strategy centers on analogy quality rubrics—faithfulness, clarity, and appropriate scope—rather than fluency alone.
detail.methods: Instruction tuning or few-shot templates with critique steps; optional tool use for symbolic checks when available.

projects.title: Vertical Los Angeles
bullets: Models the vertical distribution of skyline clusters in Los Angeles using geospatial data and machine learning.
Predicts potential locations for new high-rise clusters using density-based clustering and socioeconomic factors.

intro: Skyline “mass” is modeled as clustered vertical intensity surfaces over the LA basin, combining building height proxies with land use and economic covariates.
Downstream use is exploratory forecasting: where might the next wave of clustering emerge under similar pressures?
detail.stack: Raster or hex-grid features from building footprints / height datasets, merged with ACS-style demographics; clustering and optional gradient-boosted models for ranking candidate cells.
detail.methods: HDBSCAN / DBSCAN on engineered spatial features; calibration with held-out years or submarkets if data allows.

visuals.caption: 3D or choropleth of vertical intensity by tract (add image when ready).

projects.title: Clean Your Dishes
bullets: Detects and classifies dirty dishes in real-time using YOLO/DINO models through a camera placed in the kitchen.
Uses timestamps to track the sequence of dishes and their states, triggering an alert when a dish has been left dirty for over 30 minutes.

intro: A lightweight CV pipeline watches the sink area, classifies dish state (clean / dirty / ambiguous), and maintains a per-dish timeline from detections.
Alerts fire when dirty dwell time crosses a threshold—tunable for roommates or shared kitchens.
detail.stack: Camera stream → frame sampling → detector (YOLO or DINO-based detector) → tracker or heuristic ID stitching → timer service → notification channel.
detail.methods: Data augmentation for kitchen lighting; confidence thresholds to reduce false alarms; optional temporal smoothing across frames.

visuals.caption: Bounding boxes on sink scene + timer overlay (screenshot).
